did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used machine learning algorithms, specifically Support Vector Machine (SVM), k-nearest neighbor (KNN), random forest, and naïve bayesian classifiers, to classify skin cancer types (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma) and distinguish them from healthy tissue using diffuse reflectance spectroscopy data.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'Research Article' and presents original experimental work involving diffuse reflectance spectroscopy on 11 adult patients. It details a specific methodology (Discrete Wavelet Transform and machine learning classifiers like SVM, KNN, Random Forest), describes the data acquisition and processing steps, and reports new results including accuracy metrics (>95%) and AUC values (0.99).

title: Combined Discrete Wavelet Transform and Machine Learning from Reflectance Spectra for Screening Types of Skin Cancer in Patients
brief_summary: This study developed a hybrid signal processing approach combining Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) and machine learning to screen for skin cancer types using diffuse reflectance spectroscopy data from 11 adult patients. The researchers acquired in vivo and ex vivo reflectance spectra in the 400-1000 nm range from patients diagnosed with melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma, as well as healthy tissue. After preprocessing and feature extraction using DWT coefficients, six different classifiers including SVM, KNN, Random Forest, and Naïve Bayes were evaluated. The ensemble bagged tree method achieved the highest performance with over 98% accuracy and an AUC of 0.99, while other algorithms also demonstrated high efficacy with accuracy exceeding 95%. The results indicate that this non-invasive framework can effectively distinguish between healthy and cancerous tissues and differentiate between specific cancer subtypes.

lesion_types_studied.details: Studied in 4 patients (n=4). Classified against healthy tissue and other cancer subtypes. Recognition accuracy reported as 96.5% in first approach and ~94.8% in second approach.
Studied in 5 patients (n=5). Classified against healthy tissue and other cancer subtypes. Recognition accuracy reported as 96.7% in first approach and 100% in second approach.
Studied in 2 patients (n=2). Classified against healthy tissue and other cancer subtypes. Recognition accuracy reported as 97.9% in first approach and 100% in second approach.
Used as the negative control class for distinguishing cancerous from non-cancerous tissue. Measurements taken from healthy skin of the same patients.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer types (Malignant Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma) and healthy tissue
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification to distinguish between healthy skin and three specific cancer subtypes (MM, BCC, SCC). It also performs binary classification tasks, such as separating healthy control from cancer tissue, separating MM from other cancers, and separating BCC from SCC. The primary task is classification using discrete diagnostic categories.

datasets_used.name: In vivo and ex vivo diffuse reflectance spectroscopy dataset from Rabin Medical Center
datasets_used.description: A custom dataset comprising diffuse reflectance spectra acquired from 11 adult patients (ages 40-90) with diagnosed skin lesions at Rabin Medical Center, Beilinson Campus, Israel. Data was collected in vivo before surgery and ex vivo after surgical excision using a wideband light source (Thorlabs SLS201L), fiber-optic probe (Ocean Optics R200-7), and spectrometer (AvaSpec-ULS4096CL-EVO) over the 400-1000 nm range. Each measurement involved 100 replicate spectra co-added to a single average spectrum.

use.training: Used for training and testing machine learning classifiers (SVM, KNN, Random Forest, Naïve Bayes, etc.) after feature extraction via Discrete Wavelet Transform.
use.testing: Used for testing classifier performance, with results reported as accuracy and AUC on the same dataset (1100 data points).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of classifiers by splitting the dataset into k folds, using k-1 folds for training and one fold for validation, then averaging results across all folds.
To identify and remove spectral outliers from the raw reflectance intensity data to prevent errors in measurement.
To denoise the reflectance spectra after outlier removal.
To measure the degree of deviation between labeled experimental outcomes and true ones, and to calculate metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, TP, TN, FP, and FN.
To measure the classifier's ability to distinguish between classes, serving as a summary of the ROC curve.
To evaluate the binary classification task performance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, AUC, and confusion matrices via k-fold cross-validation.

First Approach (3-Class Classification:.notes: Overall False Negative Rate (FNR) was less than 3.5%. AUC for SCC was 0.9976. Average AUC across classes was 0.99.

Second Approach (Binary Classification:.notes: Low FN rate of less than 5.2%.
Observations: 420. All BCC/SCC types detected correctly.

Classifier Comparison (First Approach).notes: Ensemble Bagged Trees (EBT) achieved the highest average accuracy of 98%. Fine KNN had the lowest performance.

architecture_used.main_model: Multiple machine learning classifiers were evaluated: Support Vector Machine (SVM) with Gaussian, Quadratic, and Cubic kernels; k-Nearest Neighbors (KNN); Random Forest; Naïve Bayes; Fine Tree; Wide Neural Network; and Ensemble Bagged Trees (EBT). The best performing model was Ensemble Bagged Trees.
architecture_used.input_size: 12 eigenvalues (features) extracted from Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) coefficients. Specifically, 10 statistical features (standard deviation, mean, variance, median, max, min, Shannon entropy, skewness, kurtosis, power spectrum density) were calculated for each of the 5 DWT sub-bands (D1-D4 and A4), resulting in 50 total features, from which 12 were selected as input.

architecture_details.signal_processing: Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) using Daubechies wavelet function (DB4) with 5-level decomposition.
architecture_details.preprocessing: Outlier detection and removal using I_raw(λi) ≥ I_avg ± 1.96 × σ criterion; Denoising using Kaiser window filter (order 64, β=0.5).
architecture_details.feature_extraction: DWT decomposition into 5 levels (D1-D4, A4). Features included entropy, kurtosis, skewness, mean, standard deviation, variance, median, max, min, and power spectrum density. 12 eigenvalues were selected as input features.
architecture_details.svm_details: Three kernel types tested: Gaussian, Quadratic, and Cubic. SVM aims to find an optimal separating hyperplane with maximum margin.
architecture_details.tree_details: Fine Tree and Ensemble Bagged Trees (EBT) classifiers tested.

training_details.data_split: 80% training, 20% testing (initially described), but primarily evaluated using 5-fold Cross Validation (CV) where k-1 folds used for training and one fold for validation.
training_details.hyperparameters: DB4 mother wavelet for DWT; Kaiser window order 64 with β=0.5 for filtering; 5-fold CV for evaluation.

approaches: First Approach: 3-class classification (MM, BCC, SCC) and binary classification (MM vs Others).
Second Approach: Binary classification (MM vs Others) followed by binary classification (BCC vs SCC).

preprocessing_techniques: Outlier detection and removal using the criterion I_raw(λi) ≥ I_avg ± 1.96 × σ
Denoising using Kaiser window filter (order 64, β=0.5)
Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) decomposition using DB4 mother wavelet with 5-level decomposition (D1-D4 and A4 coefficients)

Feature extraction and selection (12 eigenvalues selected from statistical features including entropy, kurtosis, skewness, mean, standard deviation, variance, median, max, min, and power spectrum density)
conclusions: The hybrid framework combining Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) and machine learning classifiers effectively distinguishes between healthy skin tissue and cancerous tissues (Malignant Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma) using diffuse reflectance spectroscopy data.

The proposed method achieved high diagnostic performance with overall accuracy exceeding 95% (specifically 96.9% for the first approach and up to 98% with Ensemble Bagged Trees) and an average Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 0.99.
The false negative rate was kept low, reported as less than 3.5% in the first approach and less than 5.2% in the second approach, which is crucial for automated medical diagnosis.

The Ensemble Bagged Trees (EBT) classifier demonstrated the highest performance among the tested algorithms (including SVM, KNN, Random Forest, Naïve Bayes, Fine Tree, and Wide Neural Network), achieving an average accuracy of 98%.
In the second classification approach, the method achieved 100% accuracy in differentiating between Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC).
The study acknowledges a limitation regarding the small sample size of patients (n=11), noting that while the results indicate viability, the small number of subjects is not ideal for generalization.
The framework has the potential to support medical workers by improving the detection rate of cancerous skin lesions and increasing the efficiency of suspicious lesion assessment.
important_remarks: The study suffers from a critically small sample size, comprising only 11 adult patients (n=4 MM, n=5 BCC, n=2 SCC), which limits generalizability and reproducibility.

There is a significant risk of data leakage because 100 replicate spectra were acquired from each subject and treated as independent samples (total n=1100), rather than accounting for intra-patient correlation.
The dataset is heavily biased towards Basal Cell Carcinoma (45.5% of lesions) and Malignant Melanoma (36.4%), with Squamous Cell Carcinoma being severely underrepresented (18.2%).
The paper does not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals; performance is evaluated solely via accuracy, AUC, and confusion matrices.
External validation is absent; the study relies on a single-center dataset from Rabin Medical Center, Israel, with no independent test set or cross-institutional validation.
The age range of the cohort is narrow (50-90 years old), potentially limiting applicability to younger populations.
The paper acknowledges that the small number of patients is 'not the ideal situation' and calls for future studies with larger samples to verify results.
